title: Mayor Wu remains firm as councilors consider rejecting her budget
author: Ross Cristantiello
bullets: Mayor Wu's $4.9 billion budget features the smallest spending increase since the post-financial crisis period.
Councilors have until June 10 to accept, reject, or amend the budget, but can't increase the total spending.
Mayor Wu insists on no increased spending despite council concerns, citing fiscal responsibility and potential budget deficits.

title: Scholars bar is closing after 15 years. A new concept is already planned.
author: Autumn Sloboda
bullets: Scholars American Bistro & Cocktail Lounge in Downtown Crossing will close after its final service on Saturday.
The location at 25 School St. will reopen in late summer as Off The Rails Boston, a live music-focused venue.
The new concept will feature Americana-inspired food and a Nashville-Boston influenced setting with live programming.

title: How much are you spending to attend the World Cup?
author: Annie Jonas
bullets: Boston will host seven FIFA World Cup 2026 matches at Gillette Stadium, renamed Boston Stadium for the tournament.
Round-trip Commuter Rail tickets between South Station and Foxborough will cost $80 per person on match days.
Match-day parking near the stadium is priced around $175, with significant increases in hotel and Airbnb prices.

title: A first look at the massive outdoor bar coming to Cape Cod this summer
author: Kristi Palma
bullets: The Backyard by Foley’s, a massive 33,650 square-foot outdoor venue, is opening in Dennis Port this summer.
Features include a 5,000 square-foot sail-covered bar, picnic tables, food trucks, and kid-friendly activity zones.
The venue will offer live music, lawn games, movie nights, and collaborations with local businesses for pop-up events.

title: Chelsea man pleads not guilty after allegedly threatening BU campus, students, deans
author: Samantha Genzer
bullets: Maximillien Sajous, 29, pleaded not guilty to charges of threatening Boston University and its members.
Sajous faces stalking, bomb/hijack threat, and harassment charges and is held without bail for dangerousness.
Sajous was a BU law student and had a summer intern position rescinded by the Attorney General’s Office.

title: Healey refuses to alter RMV policy that denies undercover license plates to ICE
author: Ross Cristantiello
bullets: Governor Maura Healey maintains RMV policy denying undercover license plates to most ICE vehicles in Massachusetts.
Assistant Attorney General Brett Shumate claims RMV policy is unconstitutional, threatens legal action if not rescinded.
Healey's spokesperson states policy aims to limit ICE actions that violate rights, ensuring safety and transparency.

title: Body of Quincy kayaker pulled from Boston Harbor
author: Abby Patkin
bullets: Body of Tri Trinh, 72, was recovered from Boston Harbor after kayaking from Quincy beach.
Authorities reported no obvious signs of trauma or foul play in Trinh's death.
An autopsy will be conducted at Beth Israel Deaconess Hospital – Milton.

title: South Shore school employee pleads guilty to stealing food, equipment for ‘Snack Shack’
author: Samantha Genzer
bullets: Patrick Van Cott pleaded guilty to stealing food and equipment from Massachusetts schools to supply his Snack Shack business.
Van Cott used school and USDA funds to purchase refrigerators, freezers, burger patties, and other supplies for his business.
He may face up to 20 years in prison and $250,000 in fines for theft and wire fraud charges.

title: New Allston hotel wins design award
author: Kristi Palma
bullets: The Atlas Hotel in Allston won the Lifestyle Hotel Public Space award at the 22nd Annual Hospitality Design Awards.
Located at Harvard's Enterprise Research Campus, The Atlas Hotel opened this year with a focus on design and community.
The hotel's lobby, featuring natural materials and local art, serves as a social hub between home and work.

title: JetBlue is ending service at Manchester-Boston Regional Airport
author: Kristi Palma
bullets: JetBlue will end service at Manchester-Boston Regional Airport on July 8 due to a capacity crisis.
Airport officials expressed disappointment, citing JetBlue's decision as a response to ongoing business challenges and high fuel prices.
Impacted customers will be notified by JetBlue and can choose an alternate flight or receive a full refund.
